site.title: Blue
site.tagline: A living archive for art, school, personal experiments, and writing.
site.intro: This site collects process notes, finished work, rough drafts, and the strange little side quests that happen between them. Each category is a different room, but they all belong to the same practice.
site.about: Blue Shell Almanac is structured like a studio journal instead of a corporate portfolio. It gives projects room to show their ideas, references, setbacks, and outcomes without flattening everything into a resume.

categories.name: Art
categories.description: Sketches, finished pieces, process notes, and visual experiments.

categories.name: School
categories.description: Course projects, research notes, and academic milestones.

categories.name: Personal
categories.description: Life updates, side quests, and things worth remembering.

categories.name: Writing
categories.description: Essays, reflections, fragments, and longer-form drafts.

posts.title: Glass Bird Study
posts.summary: A color and texture study built from transparent overlays and failed first passes.
posts.body:
## Why this piece exists

I wanted to paint something delicate without making it passive. The bird shape gave me a familiar anchor, but the real point was testing how far I could push transparency before the form disappeared.

## What worked

- Layering cool shadows under warm highlights kept the surface from turning muddy.
- Photographing each stage made it easier to see when I had already solved the image.

## What changed

The first version was too literal. I scraped half of it back, kept only the wing edge, and rebuilt the composition around reflection instead of anatomy.

posts.title: Systems Class Zine
posts.summary: Turning a research-heavy assignment into a printed zine that was actually readable.
posts.body:
## Prompt

The assignment asked for a systems map. I translated mine into a short zine so the relationships between sources, people, and constraints were easier to follow.

## Structure

Each spread handled one layer of the system: actors, incentives, friction points, and possible interventions.

## Takeaway

The project taught me that formatting is not decoration. The container changes how people understand the argument.

posts.title: Desert Notes
posts.summary: Short field notes from walking at dusk and paying attention on purpose.
posts.body:
## Entry

The light was flat until the last ten minutes, then every pale rock started reflecting peach and silver at once.

I am trying to get better at recording details before they become opinions.

## Reminder

Not every useful note needs to become content. Some of them only need to sharpen the next one.

posts.title: Notes on Voice
posts.summary: A draft about building a writing voice by collecting pressures instead of chasing style.
posts.body:
## Starting point

I used to think voice was a finish you applied at the end. It feels closer to pressure now: what you notice, what you omit, what rhythm you trust, and how much risk you allow into the sentence.

## Working rule

Write closer to the real temperature of the thought. Clean it up later, but do not cool it down too early.
